title: Cherry hand mirror frame
subtitle: Sawn and pared cherry frame with rebate for a small mirror glass
excerpt: A small hand mirror frame in seasoned cherry: 20 cm by 14 cm in 18 mm cherry board, with a pared rebate on the back face to accept the mirror glass and a card backer. The cherry is finished with oil; the tight grain and warm tone need no embellishment. An intermediate project in frame-making and rebate paring.
sourceNotes: Victorian cabinet-making manuals for small frame construction; Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for woodwork and frame-making techniques.

glossaryTerms.definition: A step cut into the edge or face of a workpiece to accept a panel, glass, or lid. Also called a rabbet. Cut with a rebate plane or pared with a chisel in two passes: one across the grain, one with the grain.
An angled flat cut at an edge or corner, usually at 45 degrees. Softens edges and reduces the visual weight of a frame or panel.

content.text: Keep both hands behind the cutting edge at all times. Secure the workpiece in a vice before chiselling; do not hold it free in the hand.

Cherry is one of the most satisfying hardwoods for a small frame: the pale gold sapwood and warm reddish-brown heartwood often appear in the same blank, and the contrast is appealing. The critical joint is the
cut at the back to hold the mirror glass. Everything else is surface work.

One piece of seasoned cherry, 220 mm by 150 mm and 18 mm thick. One rectangular mirror glass, 160 mm by 100 mm. One piece of stiff card or 3 mm ply to back the glass. Four 18 mm brass screws. Two coats of oil to finish.

Mark the outer frame shape and the inner opening on the cherry blank. The inner opening must be 4 mm smaller on all sides than the mirror glass, to leave a shoulder for the rebate. Saw the outer profile and inner opening with a coping saw or jigsaw. Clean the saw marks with a sharp chisel bevel-side down, working with the grain on the long sides and using light paring cuts across the short ends.

Mark the rebate depth with a marking gauge: 4 mm deep and 4 mm wide, running around the inside edge of the opening on the back face. Chop the depth line first across the grain with the chisel, then pare out the waste in two or three passes to a flat-bottomed step. The mirror glass should sit just below the back face when placed in the rebate. Test-fit the glass before finishing.
Smooth the front face with a smoothing plane then a card scraper, working with the grain. The card scraper removes the last plane marks and leaves a surface requiring no sandpaper. Apply a light

to all four outer edges with a block plane: two passes at 45 degrees to soften the edge without reducing the visual mass of the frame. Apply two coats of oil, rubbing back lightly with 400-grit paper between coats.

Drop the mirror glass into the rebate. Cut the card backer to sit inside the opening and cover the glass. Drill and screw four small brass screws through the back face around the perimeter to retain the backer. Do not glue the glass in place, so it can be replaced if broken. Attach a picture hook or keyhole fitting to the back for hanging.

items.symptom: Rebate corner splinters when the chisel crosses the grain
items.cause: Depth line not scored before chiselling, so fibres tear beyond the boundary
items.fix: Score the depth line with a marking knife before any chisel work. The scored line severs the fibres and stops the chisel from running past the boundary into the adjacent grain.
